1657C.name: 1657C - Bracket Sequence Deletion
1657C.statement:
You are given a bracket sequence consisting of
n
n
characters '
(
' and/or
)
'. You perform several operations with it.
During one operation, you choose the
shortest
prefix of this string (some amount of first characters of the string) that is
good
and remove it from the string.
The prefix is considered
good
if one of the following two conditions is satisfied:
this prefix is a regular bracket sequence;
this prefix is a palindrome of length
at least two
.
A bracket sequence is called regular if it is possible to obtain a correct arithmetic expression by inserting characters '
+
' and '
1
' into this sequence. For example, sequences
(())()
,
()
and
(()(()))
are regular, while
)(
,
(()
and
(()))(
are not.
The bracket sequence is called palindrome if it reads the same back and forth. For example, the bracket sequences
))
,
((
and
)(()
are palindromes, while bracket sequences
()
,
)(
and
))(
are not palindromes.
You stop performing the operations when it's not possible to find a
good
prefix. Your task is to find the number of operations you will perform on the given string and the number of remaining characters in the string.
You have to answer
t
t

tutorial.Tutorial: 1657C - Bracket Sequence Deletion Consider the first character of the string. If it is ' ( ', then we can remove the first two characters of the string and continue (because the prefix of length 2 2 will be either a palindrome or a regular bracket sequence). If the first character of the string is ' ) ' then this is a bad case. Of course, the regular bracket sequence can't start with ' ( ', so this prefix should be a palindrome. And what is the shortest palindrome we can get with the first character ' ) '? It is the closing bracket ' ) ', then some (possibly, zero) amount of opening brackets ' ( ', and another one closing bracket. We can see that we can't find a palindrome shorter than this one because we have to find a pair for the first character. So, if the first character of the string is ' ) ', then we just remove anything until the next character ' ) ' inclusive. To not remove any characters explicitly, we can just use pointers instead. And the last thing is to carefully handle cases when we can't do any operations.

tutorial.solution: If a i > i a i > i for some position i i , then we need to insert at least a i − i a i − i new small elements before this position. Let m = m a x ( 0 , max i = 1 n ( a i − i ) ) m = m a x ( 0 , max i = 1 n ( a i − i ) ) . So we need at least m m operations. But its not hard to see that m m operations are enough. For example, you can insert m m 1 1 s at the beginning of the sequence. This way, all elements will be shifted by m m positions, and consequently, will satisfy that a i ≤ i a i ≤ i for each valid i i .

tutorial.solution: Let x x be the color with the highest number of candies. In case of ties, x x could be any of the candidates. Denote the number of candies with color x x as a a . Conclusion: The answer to the problem is YES if and only if there are no more than n 2 − n n 2 − n candies of color x x ; — in other words, a ≤ n 2 − n a ≤ n 2 − n holds. Proof of necessity: If there are more than n 2 − n n 2 − n candies of color x x , then there are less than n n candies not of color x x . Since there are n n rows, at least one row will have no candies not of color x x ; therefore, such a row will be filled with candies of color x x , violating the restrictions. Proof of sufficiency: If there are no more than n 2 − n n 2 − n candies of color x x , we can always construct a solution with the method below. First, if a < n a < n holds, then we can arrange the candies arbitrarily because no color has enough candies to fill a row or a column. Otherwise, n ≤ a ≤ n 2 − n n ≤ a ≤ n 2 − n holds. We can put candies of color x x in a 1 , 1 , a 2 , 2 , … , a n , n a 1 , 1 , a 2 , 2 , … , a n , n . This will take n n candies of color x x . Next, we choose n n candies not of color x x arbitrarily. We will put them at a 1 , 2 , a 2 , 3 , … , a n − 1 , n , a n , 1 a 1 , 2 , a 2 , 3 , … , a n − 1 , n , a n , 1 . Lastly, we arrange other candies that have not yet been arranged arbitrarily. Since in the first step we put a candy of color x x in each row and column, and in the second step we put a candy not of color x x in each row and column, each row and column contains at least candies of two colors, satisfying the constraints.
hints: It is always possible to rearrange the candies, unless candies of one specific color appears too often.

tutorial.solution: [problem:2209E] Firstly, some basic knowledge we need for the solution: A border of a string s s is a non-empty string t t such that | t | < | s | | t | < | s | , and t t is simultaneously a prefix and a suffix of s s . For example, both a a and aba aba are borders of abacaba abacaba ; aaa aaa is a border of aaaa aaaa . If a string does not have any borders, we call it unbordered ; otherwise, we call it bordered . For example, abaabb abaabb is unbordered. If c c is a border of b b , and b b is a border of a a , then c c is a border of a a . This can be easily derived from the definition of borders. Consider all borders of a string s s . Let them be b 1 , b 2 , … , b k b 1 , b 2 , … , b k when sorted in increasing order of their lengths. Then for every 1 ≤ i < k 1 ≤ i < k , b i b i is a border of b i + 1 b i + 1 . Now, we will derive the solution step by step. A partition will be referred to as optimal if it contains the most number of substrings. Lemma 1. For a string s s , it is either unbordered, or its shortest border is unbordered. Proof. Suppose s s is bordered, and its shortest border t t is also bordered. Then, there exists a border w w of t t , which will also be a border of s s , contradicting the fact that t t is the shortest border of s s . Lemma 2. For a string s s , there exists one and only one partition of s s into its unbordered prefixes. Proof. We shall prove by induction. Let a possible partition be s = t 1 + t 2 + … + t k s = t 1 + t 2 + … + t k . Consider two cases: If s s is unbordered, the only possible partition is s s itself. This is because the last substring, t k t k , must either be s s or a border of s s (prefix by definition of partition, suffix by being the last substring). Otherwise, since t k t k is an unbordered border of s s , it is the shortest border of s s . Thus, the last substring of the partition is fixed. This reduces the problem to partitioning t 1 + t 2 + … + t k − 1 t 1 + t 2 + … + t k − 1 . Corollary 3. The unique partition of s s into its unbordered prefixes can be obtained by iteratively trimming the shortest border of s s until s s is unbordered. Lemma 4. In an optimal partition of s s into its prefixes ( not necessarily unbordered ), all substrings must be unbordered. Proof. If any t i t i in the partition of s s is bordered, we can partition it further into the unbordered prefixes of t i t i , which will also be unbordered prefixes of s s . Theorem. The unique optimal partition of s s into its prefixes can be obtained by iteratively trimming the shortest border of s s until s s is unbordered. Proof. All substrings in the optimal partition are unbordered (Lemma 4) → → There exists a unique partition into unbordered prefixes (Lemma 2) → → This partition can be obtained by iteratively trimming the shortest border (Corollary 3). Now we will discuss how to implement this algorithm efficiently. Notice that the constraints allow a O ( n q ) O ( n q ) solution, so we can treat each query independently. Thus, we will discuss how to solve the problem for a single string s s . Firstly, compute the π π array of s s using KMP, where π i π i is the length of the longest border of s [ 1.. i ] s [ 1.. i ] . However, instead of the longest border, we need the shortest one. Let b i b i be the length of the shortest border of s [ 1.. i ] s [ 1.. i ] , or 0 0 if no border exists. b b can also be easily computed by a simple linear scan: b i = ⎧ ⎩ ⎨ 0 π i b π i π i = 0 π π i = 0 otherwise b i = { 0 π i = 0 π i π π i = 0 b π i otherwise Lastly, the f f value of all prefixes of s s can be computed by another linear scan. Let d p i = f ( s [ 1.. i ] ) d p i = f ( s [ 1.. i ] ) : d p i = { 1 d p i − b i + 1 b i = 0 otherwise d p i = { 1 b i = 0 d p i − b i + 1 otherwise As such, we have solved the problem in O ( n q ) O ( n q ) time.

tutorial.solution: [problem:2209A] It's sure that OtterZ will kill as many monsters as he can. To make use of flip flops, OtterZ will keep throwing flip flops if he has flip flops left and the monsters' combat power is strictly weaker ( c > a i c > a i ). A simple algorithm can be O ( T n 2 ) O ( T n 2 ) ; if you want to make it faster, you can consider that the weakest monster that remains is always the one that can be killed if there are still some monsters that can be killed, then sort all the monsters and kill them one by one. This leads to a faster algorithm in O ( T n log n ) O ( T n log ⁡ n ) .
tutorial.Tutorial: Yes, I think the adaptive interactor wasn't the best choice, and they probably should have covered the simple case themselves. For example, my solution like 367690402 passes when it really should not. Realized it was wrong mid-explanation while streaming T-T.
tutorial.Editorial: We hope you enjoyed the problems! Rate the contest! Quality 44 Excellent contest 112 Good contest 28 Average contest 6 Bad contest 40 Horrible contest Difficulty 3 Trivial contest 20 Easy contest 109 Average contest 76 Hard contest 4 Impossible contest [problem:2209A] Idea: OtterZ Solution: OtterZ Editorial: OtterZ Solution [problem:2209A] It's sure that OtterZ will kill as many monsters as he can. To make use of flip flops, OtterZ will keep throwing flip flops if he has flip flops left and the monsters' combat power is strictly weaker ( c > a i c > a i ). A simple algorithm can be O ( T n 2 ) O ( T n 2 ) ; if you want to make it faster, you can consider that the weakest monster that remains is always the one that can be killed if there are still some monsters that can be killed, then sort all the monsters and kill them one by one. This leads to a faster algorithm in O ( T n log n ) O ( T n log ⁡ n ) . Code (C++) #include < bits / stdc ++. h > using namespace std ; int n , a [ 109 ], k ; long long C ; int main (){ int t ; scanf ( "%d" ,& t ); while ( t --){ scanf ( "%d %lld %d" ,& n ,& C ,& k ); for ( int i = 1 ; i <= n ; i ++){ scanf ( "%d" ,& a [ i ]); } sort ( a + 1 , a + n + 1 ); for ( int i = 1 ; i <= n ; i ++){ if ( a [ i ] > C ) break ; int o = min ( 1ll * k , C - a [ i ]); k -= o ; C += a [ i ] + o ; } printf ( "%lld\n" , C ); } } Rate the problem! Quality 44 Excellent problem 103 Good problem 30 Average problem 8 Bad problem 15 Horrible problem Difficulty 27 Trivial problem 109 Easy problem 27 Average problem 9 Hard problem 3 Impossible problem [problem:2209B] Idea: OtterZ Solution: OtterZ Editorial: OtterZ Solution [problem:2209B] As | a i − k | > | a j − k | | a i − k | > | a j − k | leads to k < a i + a j 2 , a i > a j k < a i + a j 2 , a i > a j or k > a i + a j 2 , a i < a j k > a i + a j 2 , a i < a j . It is clear that we should choose k k to be either much larger than the maximum value or much smaller than the minimum value. Then we need to compute: f 1 i = ∑ n j = i + 1 [ a i > a j ] , f 2 i = ∑ n j = i + 1 [ a i < a j ] f 1 i = ∑ j = i + 1 n [ a i > a j ] , f 2 i = ∑ j = i + 1 n [ a i < a j ] Which can be done using bruteforce in O ( n 2 ) O ( n 2 ) or Fenwick tree in O ( n log n ) O ( n log ⁡ n ) . Code (Python) for _ in range ( int ( input ())): n = int ( input ()) a = list ( map ( int , input (). split ())) print (*[ max ( sum ( a [ j ] < a [ i ] for j in range ( i + 1 , n )), sum ( a [ j ] > a [ i ] for j in range ( i + 1 , n ))) for i in range ( n )]) Code (C++) #include < bits / stdc ++. h > using namespace std ; int n , a [ 5009 ], bcnt , f1 [ 5009 ], f2 [ 5009 ]; int main (){ int t ; scanf ( "%d" ,& t ); while ( t --){ scanf ( "%d" ,& n ); for ( int i = 1 ; i <= n ; i ++){ scanf ( "%d" ,& a [ i ]); } for ( int i = n ; i >= 1 ; i --){ f1 [ i ] = f2 [ i ] = 0 ; for ( int j = i + 1 ; j <= n ; j ++) f1 [ i ] += ( a [ i ] > a [ j ]), f2 [ i ] += ( a [ i ] < a [ j ]); f1 [ i ] = max ( f1 [ i ], f2 [ i ]); } for ( int i = 1 ; i < n ; i ++) printf ( "%d " , f1 [ i ]); printf ( "%d\n" , f1 [ n ]); } } Rate the problem! Quality 44 Excellent problem 71 Good problem 37 Average problem 9 Bad problem 35 Horrible problem Difficulty 26 Trivial problem 103 Easy problem 35 Average problem 9 Hard problem 2 Impossible problem [problem:2209C] Idea: __baozii__ Solution: __baozii__ Editorial: __baozii__ Solution [problem:2209C] Firstly, the specific values of integers greater than 0 0 do not matter as the only case where a i = a j a i = a j is when both a i a i and a j a j are zero. Consider the following strategy: For every pair ( 2 i − 1 , 2 i ) ( 2 i − 1 , 2 i ) such that 1 ≤ i ≤ n 1 ≤ i ≤ n , we query ( 2 i − 1 , 2 i ) ( 2 i − 1 , 2 i ) . If any pair is equal, we are done. This phase uses at most n n queries. Otherwise, since the array contains exactly n n zeroes, there must be exactly one zero in every pair. We now query if ( 1 , 3 ) ( 1 , 3 ) and ( 1 , 4 ) ( 1 , 4 ) . Similarly, if any of them returns 1 1 , we are done. Otherwise, a 1 a 1 must be non-zero, which implies that a 2 a 2 must be zero. This phase uses at most 2 2 queries. The above strategy is correct, but it uses n + 2 n + 2 queries rather than n + 1 n + 1 . Are any of the queries redundant? In fact, yes. We actually do not need to query ( 1 , 2 ) ( 1 , 2 ) at all! To prove this fact, we shall do some casework. Since at any point in time, we are done if we receive a 1 1 from the judge, we can assume that all queries have returned a 0 0 : a 1 ≠ 0 a 1 ≠ 0 , a 2 ≠ 0 a 2 ≠ 0 : This implies that there are n n zeroes among the remaining 2 n − 2 2 n − 2 elements. By the pigeonhole principle, there must be at least one pair ( 2 i − 1 , 2 i ) ( 2 i − 1 , 2 i ) such that a 2 i − 1 = a 2 i = 0 a 2 i − 1 = a 2 i = 0 . However, this is impossible as all queries have returned 0 0 . a 1 = 0 a 1 = 0 , a 2 ≠ 0 a 2 ≠ 0 : This implies that a 3 ≠ 0 a 3 ≠ 0 and a 4 ≠ 0 a 4 ≠ 0 , as we queried ( 1 , 3 ) ( 1 , 3 ) and ( 1 , 4 ) ( 1 , 4 ) . 275B.name: 275B - Convex Shape
275B.statement:
Consider an
n
×
m
grid. Initially all the cells of the grid are colored white. Lenny has painted some of the cells (at least one) black. We call a painted grid
convex
if one can walk from
any
black cell to
any another
black cell using a path of side-adjacent black cells changing his direction at most once during the path. In the figure below, the left grid is convex while the right one is not convex, because there exist two cells which need more than one time to change direction in their path.
You're given a painted grid in the input. Tell Lenny if the grid is convex or not.
Input
The first line of the input contains two integers
n
and
m
(1 ≤
n
,
m
≤ 50)
— the size of the grid. Each of the next
n
lines contains
m
characters "
B
" or "
W
". Character "
B
" denotes a black cell of the grid and "
W
" denotes a white cell of the grid.
It's guaranteed that the grid has at least one black cell.
Output
On the only line of the output print "
YES
" if the grid is convex, otherwise print "
NO
". Do not print quotes.
Examples
Input
Copy
3 4
WWBW
BWWW
WWWB
Output
Copy
NO
Input
Copy
3 1
B
B
W
Output
Copy
YES

2194C.name: 2194C - Secret message
2194C.statement:
Bob, the genius spy, has intercepted an encrypted message. He assumes that it contains secret information and is actively engaged in deciphering it.
The note, which has fallen into the hands of the spy, consists of
k
k
strips, each of length
n
n
and containing strict lowercase Latin letters. With extensive experience in deciphering such documents, Bob guessed that the message he is interested in (the deciphering of the note) is also a string of length
n
n
, and the
i
i
-th letter of this message corresponds to the
i
i
-th letter of one of the strips.
According to Bob,
the informativity
of a string
s
s
is defined as the minimum positive integer
d
d
such that a string
t
t
of length
d
d
exists, and
s
s
can be formed by repeating
t
t
several times. For example, the informativity of the string "
aaaa
" is 1, the informativity of the string "
abab
" is 2, and the informativity of the string "
abcd
" is 4.
Bob assumes that the note's author, to ensure reliable transmission of information, repeated the data in the message several times. Therefore, he believes that a more plausible decryption of the note would have the smallest possible informativity.
Help the spy: find the message that represents the decryption of the note and has the minimum possible informativity.
Input
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases
t

≤

In each of the following
k

tutorial.solution: Let's iterate over d d (divisor of n n ) and check if we can obtain a string with informativeness d d . For this, for each 1 ≤ i ≤ d 1 ≤ i ≤ d , we need to check that there is a common letter at positions i , i + d , i + 2 d , … i , i + d , i + 2 d , … . To do this, we will iterate over the letter at position i i and check that this letter is also present at positions i + d , i + 2 d , … i + d , i + 2 d , … . For position i i , the check can be done in O ( n d ⋅ k 2 ) O ( n d ⋅ k 2 ) , and for all i i , it will work in O ( n k 2 ) O ( n k 2 ) . In total, across all d d , the algorithm will run in O ( n ⋅ d ( n ) ⋅ k 2 ) O ( n ⋅ d ( n ) ⋅ k 2 ) (where d ( n ) d ( n ) is the number of divisors of the number n n , which does not exceed 100 100 in the problem constraints). With careful implementation, such a solution can pass all tests, but it can be improved. The idea: instead of storing k k letters at each position, let's store an array of length 26, where for each letter it is recorded whether it is present at this position or not. Now, for each i i , we can check in O ( 26 ⋅ n d ) O ( 26 ⋅ n d ) whether there is a common letter at positions i , i + d , i + 2 d , ⋯ i , i + d , i + 2 d , ⋯ (iterate over one of the letters at position i i and check that it fits for n d n d ). Thus, the overall asymptotic complexity of the algorithm will be O ( 26 ⋅ n ⋅ d ( n ) ) O ( 26 ⋅ n ⋅ d ( n ) ) .

339C.name: 339C - Xenia and Weights
339C.statement:
Xenia has a set of weights and pan scales. Each weight has an integer weight from 1 to 10 kilos. Xenia is going to play with scales and weights a little. For this, she puts weights on the scalepans, one by one. The first weight goes on the left scalepan, the second weight goes on the right scalepan, the third one goes on the left scalepan, the fourth one goes on the right scalepan and so on. Xenia wants to put the total of
m
weights on the scalepans.
Simply putting weights on the scales is not interesting, so Xenia has set some rules. First, she does not put on the scales two consecutive weights of the same weight. That is, the weight that goes
i
-th should be different from the
(
i
+ 1)
-th weight for any
i
(1 ≤
i
<
m
)
. Second, every time Xenia puts a weight on some scalepan, she wants this scalepan to outweigh the other one. That is, the sum of the weights on the corresponding scalepan must be strictly greater than the sum on the other pan.
You are given all types of weights available for Xenia. You can assume that the girl has an infinite number of weights of each specified type. Your task is to help Xenia lay
m
weights on ​​the scales or to say that it can't be done.
Input
The first line contains a string consisting of exactly ten zeroes and ones: the
i
-th
(
i
≥ 1)
character in the line equals "
1
" if Xenia has
i
kilo weights, otherwise the character equals "
0
". The second line contains integer
m
(1 ≤
m
≤ 1000)
.
Output
In the first line print "
YES
", if there is a way to put
m
weights on the scales by all rules. Otherwise, print in the first line "
NO
". If you can put
m
weights on the scales, then print in the next line
m
integers — the weights' weights in the order you put them on the scales.
If there are multiple solutions, you can print any of them.
Examples
Input
Copy
0000000101
3
Output
Copy
YES
8 10 8
Input
Copy
1000000000
2
Output
Copy
NO

tutorial.solution: For simplicity, let's denote f ( a [ l , r ] ) f ( a [ l , r ] ) as f ( l , r ) f ( l , r ) . First, notice that f ( l , r ) ≤ f ( l , r + 1 ) f ( l , r ) ≤ f ( l , r + 1 ) . This is trivially true because each unique element that appears in the range ( l , r ) ( l , r ) must appear in ( l , r + 1 ) ( l , r + 1 ) also. Now, f ( l , r + 1 ) f ( l , r + 1 ) is either equal to f ( l , r ) f ( l , r ) if a r + 1 a r + 1 already appeared, or f ( l , r ) + 1 f ( l , r ) + 1 if a r + 1 a r + 1 does not appear in [ l , r ] [ l , r ] . Now, expanding b i − b i − 1 b i − b i − 1 yields f ( i , i ) + ( f ( i − 1 , i ) − f ( i − 1 , i − 1 ) ) + ( f ( i − 2 , i ) − f ( i − 2 , i − 1 ) ) + … + ( f ( 1 , i ) − f ( 1 , i − 1 ) ) f ( i , i ) + ( f ( i − 1 , i ) − f ( i − 1 , i − 1 ) ) + ( f ( i − 2 , i ) − f ( i − 2 , i − 1 ) ) + … + ( f ( 1 , i ) − f ( 1 , i − 1 ) ) . From the above, we know that f ( l , i ) ≠ f ( l , i − 1 ) f ( l , i ) ≠ f ( l , i − 1 ) only when a i a i does not appear in [ l , i − 1 ] [ l , i − 1 ] . Let x x be the largest integer such that f ( x , i − 1 ) = f ( x , i ) f ( x , i − 1 ) = f ( x , i ) . Note that this implies that a x = a i a x = a i . For all y > x y > x , we have f ( y , i ) − f ( y , i − 1 ) = 1 f ( y , i ) − f ( y , i − 1 ) = 1 . Thus, we have b i − b i − 1 = i − ( i − x ) = x b i − b i − 1 = i − ( i − x ) = x , because all terms of the expansion above are 1 1 except for the ones corresponding to indices x x to i − 1 i − 1 . Since we know that a x = a i a x = a i , and x = b i − b i − 1 x = b i − b i − 1 , we should set a i a i to be the same as a b i − b i − 1 a b i − b i − 1 . There is one more case where x x is undefined, because f ( y , i ) ≠ f ( y , i − 1 ) f ( y , i ) ≠ f ( y , i − 1 ) for all y ≥ 1 y ≥ 1 . This implies a i a i did not appear before in the array. We can just set a i a i to any unused number arbitrarily.

112A.name: 112A - Petya and Strings
112A.statement:
Little Petya loves presents. His mum bought him two strings of the same size for his birthday. The strings consist of uppercase and lowercase Latin letters. Now Petya wants to compare those two strings
lexicographically
. The letters' case does not matter, that is an uppercase letter is considered equivalent to the corresponding lowercase letter. Help Petya perform the comparison.
Input
Each of the first two lines contains a bought string. The strings' lengths range from
1
to
100
inclusive. It is guaranteed that the strings are of the same length and also consist of uppercase and lowercase Latin letters.
Output
If the first string is less than the second one, print "-1". If the second string is less than the first one, print "1". If the strings are equal, print "0". Note that the letters' case is not taken into consideration when the strings are compared.
Examples
Input
Copy
aaaa
aaaA
Output
Copy
0
Input
Copy
abs
Abz
Output
Copy
-1
Input
Copy
abcdefg
AbCdEfF
Output
Copy
1
Note
If you want more formal information about the lexicographical order (also known as the "
dictionary order
" or "
alphabetical order
"), you can visit the following site:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lexicographical_order

tutorial.solution: Here you can find solutions to the problems from the past round. Editorial for Problem D (Div 1) was prepared by sdya . Division 2, problem A In this problem one could transform all letters in both strings to lower case and then compare the strings lexicographically. Division 2, problem B One can notice that if we want to divide a square into two equal parts, then the cutting line should pass through the center of our square. Thus, if the marked cell contains the center of the square, then we can’t make a cut, otherwise we can. Here is the code which solves the problem: scanf( "%d%d%d" , &n, &x, &y); n /= 2; if ((x == n || x == n + 1) && (y == n || y == n + 1)) printf( "NO\n" ); else printf( "YES\n" ); Division 2, problem C ( Division 1, problem A ) It is easy to see that in order to maximize the sum of squares, one should make all numbers except the first one equal to 1 and maximize the first number. Keeping this in mind we only need to check whether the given value of y is large enough to satisfy a restriction that all n numbers are positive. If y is not to small, then all we need is to ensure that x ≤ 1 + 1 + … + ( y - ( n - 1)) 2 Division 2, Problem D (Divison 1, problem B) Let’s create an array used[], j -th element of which will be the index of the last number from the input, which is divisible by j . Then for each query we’ll iterate over all divisors of x i and for each k, which divides x i we’ll check whether it is “unique”. After that we’ll update used[k]. Division 2, Problem E (Division 1, problem C) This problem has many different approaches. One of them uses the fact that the overall number of possible inputs is small and it is possible to compute the answer manually for all of them. One could also write a brute-force with a few optimizations, which works even without a precalc. However, the major part of all solutions involved dynamic programming with bitmasks. The solution below was described by Zlobober. Instead of counting the maximal number of free cells, we’ll count the minimal number of occupied cells. We’ll assume that the number of rows is not greater than 6 (otherwise we can rotate the board). Let D[k][pmask][mask] be the minimal number of occupied cells in the first k columns with the restrictions that the k-th column is described by pmask (ones correspond to occupied cells and zeroes correspond to free cells) and k+1-st column is described by mask. To make a transition from D[k-1][*][*] we can iterate over all possible masks for the k-1-st column, check whether we can distribute spiders in kth column knowing the masks for k+1-st and k-1-st columns and find the minimal value of D[k-1][*][pmask] for all such masks. The overall complexity is O(n*2 3m ), n > m. Division 1, Problem D One can notice that if m = 1 then the answer is k n , because all colorings are possible. Now we’ll assume that m > 1. Let’s look on the first column of the board (i.e. the vertical cut will be made right next to the first column). Suppose there are x distinct colors in this column. Then in the rest of the board there are also x colors. If we move the vertical line by one unit to the right, the number of different colors to the left of it will not decrease and the number of colors to the right of it won’t increase. It means that the number of different colors in both parts of the board will be also x . We can repeat this process until the line reaches the rightmost column, which means that the number of distinct colors in it is also x . It is easy to see that we can only use colors which belong to the intersection of sets of colors in the leftmost and rightmost columns in the rest of the board. Let’s iterate over all values of x and y , where x is the number of colors in the leftmost column and y is the number of elements in intersection of sets of colors in the rightmost and leftmost columns. It is easy to see that x is limited by the number of rows in the board and y can’t be greater than x . Let’s find the answer for all suc
tutorial.Editorial: Here you can find solutions to the problems from the past round. Editorial for Problem D (Div 1) was prepared by sdya . Division 2, problem A In this problem one could transform all letters in both strings to lower case and then compare the strings lexicographically. Division 2, problem B One can notice that if we want to divide a square into two equal parts, then the cutting line should pass through the center of our square. Thus, if the marked cell contains the center of the square, then we can’t make a cut, otherwise we can. Here is the code which solves the problem: scanf( "%d%d%d" , &n, &x, &y); n /= 2; if ((x == n || x == n + 1) && (y == n || y == n + 1)) printf( "NO\n" ); else printf( "YES\n" ); Division 2, problem C ( Division 1, problem A ) It is easy to see that in order to maximize the sum of squares, one should make all numbers except the first one equal to 1 and maximize the first number. Keeping this in mind we only need to check whether the given value of y is large enough to satisfy a restriction that all n numbers are positive. If y is not to small, then all we need is to ensure that x ≤ 1 + 1 + … + ( y - ( n - 1)) 2 Division 2, Problem D (Divison 1, problem B) Let’s create an array used[], j -th element of which will be the index of the last number from the input, which is divisible by j . Then for each query we’ll iterate over all divisors of x i and for each k, which divides x i we’ll check whether it is “unique”. After that we’ll update used[k]. Division 2, Problem E (Division 1, problem C) This problem has many different approaches. One of them uses the fact that the overall number of possible inputs is small and it is possible to compute the answer manually for all of them. One could also write a brute-force with a few optimizations, which works even without a precalc. However, the major part of all solutions involved dynamic programming with bitmasks. The solution below was described by Zlobober. Instead of counting the maximal number of free cells, we’ll count the minimal number of occupied cells. We’ll assume that the number of rows is not greater than 6 (otherwise we can rotate the board). Let D[k][pmask][mask] be the minimal number of occupied cells in the first k columns with the restrictions that the k-th column is described by pmask (ones correspond to occupied cells and zeroes correspond to free cells) and k+1-st column is described by mask. To make a transition from D[k-1][*][*] we can iterate over all possible masks for the k-1-st column, check whether we can distribute spiders in kth column knowing the masks for k+1-st and k-1-st columns and find the minimal value of D[k-1][*][pmask] for all such masks. The overall complexity is O(n*2 3m ), n > m. Division 1, Problem D One can notice that if m = 1 then the answer is k n , because all colorings are possible. Now we’ll assume that m > 1. Let’s look on the first column of the board (i.e. the vertical cut will be made right next to the first column). Suppose there are x distinct colors in this column. Then in the rest of the board there are also x colors. If we move the vertical line by one unit to the right, the number of different colors to the left of it will not decrease and the number of colors to the right of it won’t increase. It means that the number of different colors in both parts of the board will be also x . We can repeat this process until the line reaches the rightmost column, which means that the number of distinct colors in it is also x . It is easy to see that we can only use colors which belong to the intersection of sets of colors in the leftmost and rightmost columns in the rest of the board. Let’s iterate over all values of x and y , where x is the number of colors in the leftmost column and y is the number of elements in intersection of sets of colors in the rightmost and leftmost columns. It is easy to see that x is limited by the number of rows in the board and y can’t be greater than x . Let’s find the answer for all such pairs of x and y and at the end we’ll add them up together. Suppose x and y are fixed. We first need to choose (2x - y) colors from the given k colors, which we will use, which means that the answer for will be multiplied by C(k, 2x — y) . After that we’ll choose (x-y) unique colors which will be used in the first column, which means that the answer will be also multiplied by C(2x-y, x-y) . Then we’ll choose x-y colors for the rightmost column and multiply the answer by C(x, x-y) . Now all we need to know is how many ways of coloring n cells into x colors are there. We’ll use a dynamic programming approach to solve this sub-problem. Let d[i][j] be the number of ways to color a rectangle of unit width and length i into colors, numerated from 1 to j with the following restriction: if a < b then the first appearence of color a in the rectangle will be before the first appearence of color b . Then we can calculate this function using the following recurrence: d[i][j] = j * d[i — 1][j] +

1846E1.name: 1846E1 - Rudolf and Snowflakes (simple version)
1846E1.statement:
This is a simple version of the problem. The only difference is that in this version
n
≤
10
6
n
≤
10
6
.
One winter morning, Rudolf was looking thoughtfully out the window, watching the falling snowflakes. He quickly noticed a certain symmetry in the configuration of the snowflakes. And like a true mathematician, Rudolf came up with a mathematical model of a snowflake.
He defined a snowflake as an undirected graph constructed according to the following rules:
Initially, the graph has only one vertex.
Then, more vertices are added to the graph. The initial vertex is connected by edges to
k
k
new vertices (
k
>
1
k
>
1
).
Each vertex that is connected to only one other vertex is connected by edges to
k
k
more new vertices. This step should be done
at least once
.
The smallest possible snowflake for
k
=
4
k
=
4
is shown in the figure.
After some mathematical research, Rudolf realized that such snowflakes may not have any number of vertices. Help Rudolf check if a snowflake with
n
n
vertices can exist.
Input
The first line of the input contains an integer
t
t
(
1
≤
t
≤
10
4
1
≤
t
≤
10
4
) — the number of test cases.
Then follow the descriptions of the test cases.
The first line of each test case contains an integer
n
n
(
1
≤
n
≤
10
6
1
≤
n
≤
10
6
) — the number of vertices for which it is necessary to check the existence of a snowflake.
Output
Output
t
t
lines, each of which is the answer to the corresponding test case — "YES" if there exists such
k
>
1
k
>
1
for which a snowflake with the given number of vertices can be constructed; "NO" otherwise.
Example
Input
Copy
9
1
2
3
6
13
15
255
10101
1000000
Output
Copy
NO
NO
NO
NO
YES
YES
YES
YES
NO

tutorial.solution: #include <iostream> using namespace std ; int main () { int test_cases ; cin >> test_cases ; for ( int test_case = 0 ; test_case < test_cases ; test_case ++) { int n ; cin >> n ; int ans = 0 ; for ( int i = 0 ; i < n ; i ++) { int a , b ; cin >> a >> b ; if ( a > b ) ans ++; } cout << ans << endl ; } return 0 ; }

tutorial.Tutorial: 1846A - Rudolph and Cut the Rope In order for the candy to be on the ground, it is necessary that all the ropes touch the ground. This means that the length of all ropes must be greater than or equal to the height of the nails to which they are attached. That is, you need to cut all the ropes, the length of which is less than the height of their nail. Then the answer is equal to the number of elements that have a i > b i a i > b i .
